retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation of Postmortem Changes -- Issues of Concern -- Stages of Decomposition
retrieved_docs.content: The earliest external sign of putrefaction is the greenish discoloration of the skin of the anterior abdominal wall in the right iliac fossa region. In this region of the abdomen, the caecum, loaded with semi-solid intestinal content and commensal gut bacteria, lies quite superficial. This greenish discoloration of the skin results from the formation of sulfhemoglobin facilitated by the commensal intestinal bacteria that invade the tissues after death. This skin discoloration in the right iliac fossa region appears about 18 hours after death. In temperate climates, this greening may first appear 2 to 3 days after death. The ambient temperature affects the speed of onset of putrefaction and its rate of progression.

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation of Postmortem Changes -- Issues of Concern -- Decomposition
retrieved_docs.content: Unlike autolytic changes, putrefactive changes are visible on a macroscopic level as discoloration of the skin or bloating of body parts such as the face, abdomen, breast, and scrotum. It can appear in various forms, such as putrefactive fluids and putrefactive gases.

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation of Postmortem Changes -- Issues of Concern -- Stages of Decomposition
retrieved_docs.content: Active decay is a stage where putrefaction accelerates after bloating. Postmortem purging where putrefactive body fluids become forced out of body orifices is observable during this stage of decomposition. The detachment of hair or hair sloughing and black discoloration of ruptured skin are seen.

retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, gastric acid denatures peroxidase, so ingesting raw vegetables should not be problematic in patients with normal gastric acid secretion. [46] Ingestion of 750 g of raw, peroxidase-rich fruit and vegetables daily can cause false-positive results; this is an unusually large amount of vegetation to be eaten daily. [47] While delaying the development of the slide by 48 hours will reduce interferences from plant peroxidases, many manufacturers of guaiac-based tests recommend excluding high-peroxidase-containing fruits and vegetables before and during the collection of samples. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Death and Dying(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to identify how to know death has occurred and to educate the family of a patient who may be actively dying. This is especially important if the patient is choosing to die at home. No breathing No heartbeat No response to verbal or tactile stimuli Loss of bowels or bladder Eyelids slightly open; eyes fixed on a certain spot; no pupil activity Jaw relaxed and mouth slightly open [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Methods of Estimation of Time Since Death -- Function -- Immediate PMI
retrieved_docs.content: In the immediate period, the body undergoes rapid biochemical and physiological changes that are primarily caused by the absence of circulation of blood and loss of regulatory mechanisms. These changes are principally detectable in the eyes and the skin. In the eyes, 'trucking' or segmentation of retinal blood vessels is one of the first observable signs. This sign presents as a break in the continual column of blood on ophthalmoscopic examination of the eyes and usually occurs within half an hour and may sometimes take as long as 2 hours after death. [1] The other changes in the eyes, in the immediate post-mortem phase, include loss of intraocular pressure and the clouding of the cornea. The intraocular pressure decreases drastically after death and reaches 4 mmHg or less within 6 hours after death. [2] The cornea begins to cloud within 2 hours after death and usually prevents intra-ocular examination with an ophthalmoscope. [3] The skin loses its elasticity and luster within the first few hours after death and appears pale. Histological examination of the skin, however, shows no morphological changes within 6 hours PMI. [4] Other examinations show a lack of cellular or biochemical changes within 3 to 6 hours post-mortem. [5] Emptying gastric contents is another method used for estimating the post-mortem interval. Small light meals get emptied from the stomach within 1 to 3 hours, and the time of consumption, if known – along with volume and type of meal, can be used to estimate the post-mortem interval. [6] Therefore, the immediate post-mortem phase can be termed as the post-mortem interval between somatic and cellular death, within 2 to 3 hours after death, and usually denotes a lack of discernible changes in the morphology or histo-chemistry.

retrieved_docs.title: Postmortem Changes -- Issues of Concern -- Classification of Post Mortem Changes
retrieved_docs.content: The first external sign of putrefaction is the greenish discoloration of the skin over the right iliac fossa, the region overlying the caecum, the contents of which are mostly liquid and are full of bacteria. The greenish discoloration gradually spreads to the abdomen and involves the entire body in later stages. The bacteria generate hydrogen sulfide as a result of reductive catalysis. Hydrogen sulfide reacts with the hemoglobin and forms sulphahemoglobin that stains the surrounding region green. [33] Microbes traverse throughout the body using the blood vessels. Putrefaction of erythrocytes within the superficial blood vessels leads to the formation of greenish-blue discoloration, which is observable through the skin. This outlining of the superficial blood vessels is known as ‘marbling’ of the skin. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation of Postmortem Changes -- Issues of Concern -- Decomposition
retrieved_docs.content: Two mechanisms are involved in decomposition: autolysis and putrefaction. [6] Although decomposition begins soon after death through autolysis, the macroscopic changes brought about by decomposition become apparent much later when putrefaction occurs.

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation of Postmortem Changes -- Issues of Concern -- Early Postmortem Changes
retrieved_docs.content: The eye changes during the early postmortem period include corneal turbidity/opacity and tache noire formation. The intraocular tension progressively falls to nil about 2 hours after death, although this is disputable.

retrieved_docs.title: Bleach Toxicity(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Eyes: Commonly reddened, irritated, and tearing. Vision may be blurry. Mouth/throat: Irritation is normal to the examiner. Absent other caustic ingestants, the mouth, and throat are resistant to damage. Skin: Mild irritation may be evident. Do not let the patient spread the chemicals. Stomach/GI tract: These are rare symptoms. The GI tract is resistant and resilient. Respiratory: Irritation, including bronchospasm, can occur. This does not apply to chlorine gas exposure, a much more severe problem. Exposure to chlorine gas can be fatal. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Postmortem Changes -- Issues of Concern -- Classification of Post Mortem Changes
retrieved_docs.content: The lividity does not appear on the parts of the body which are in contact with the floor/ bed/ slab, etc., or those compressed due to tight clothing, bands, etc., because of the obstruction and compression effect of surface in the underlying vessels. The absence of discoloration in these areas is called ‘contact flattening,’ ‘contact pallor,’ or ‘contact blanching.’

retrieved_docs.title: Postmortem Changes -- Issues of Concern -- Classification of Post Mortem Changes
retrieved_docs.content: The appearance of postmortem staining depends on multiple factors, including, amount of blood at the time of death and the coagulability status, etc. Lividity is feeble/ less marked in deaths following blood loss/ in anaemics, while it is more intense in cases of asphyxia deaths. Post mortem staining can be of a distinct color in cases of poisoning. Cherry red staining presents in carbon monoxide poisoning, pink-colored staining is observable in cyanide poisoning, and brown colored staining occurs in nitrate poisoning. [28] [29] [30] Thus, postmortem staining, besides PMI, can provide useful information on the position of the body, or even the cause of death. Late post mortem changes: Autolysis:

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation of Postmortem Changes -- Issues of Concern -- Stages of Decomposition
retrieved_docs.content: In the bloated stage, body parts, including organs and soft tissues, swelling due to the accumulation of putrefactive gases or other decompositional products from the putrefaction process. It usually starts in the abdomen and then slowly affects other parts, including the face, breasts, and genitals. [11] [12] Also, during this stage, skin changes occur, such as blisters and slippage. Skin slippage at the extremities is known as degloving. Moreover, the phenomenon of marbling is also present during this stage, where blood vessels are visible on the skin as greenish-black streaks and eventually results in skin discoloration ranging from green to black. [4] These postmortem changes are apparent in about 24 to 48 hours after death.

retrieved_docs.title: Case Study: 32-Year-Old Male Presenting with Right Lower Quadrant Abdominal Pain (Archived) -- Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Although Meckel's diverticulum is usually asymptomatic and may never be identified until autopsy reports, it can lead to serious complications, most notably hemorrhage, obstruction, perforation, or infection. Hemorrhage occurs secondary to acid secretion from the gastric mucosa that forms ulcerations along the intestinal mucosa and can lead to painless rectal bleeding, typically in children. Bleeding can be substantial and may require transfusions. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Bleach Toxicity(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Mouth/throat: In the home and emergency department, give plenty of water to drink. Milk may be more soothing but not necessarily. Stomach/GI tract: At home and in the emergency department, do not induce vomiting; if the bleached burned on the way down, it would burn on the way back up. Give plenty of water.

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation of Postmortem Changes -- Issues of Concern -- Stages of Decomposition
retrieved_docs.content: There are five stages of decomposition, namely fresh, bloated, active decay, advanced decay, and skeletal stage. [10] These stages may occur simultaneously in different parts of the same corpse, and it may be difficult for a forensic pathologist to label the state of the corpse with a single stage. [1] The fresh stage is the immediate period following death in which autolysis occurs. Algor mortis, livor mortis, and rigor mortis are apparent in this stage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2692
retrieved_docs.content: The qualifier “chemically induced” can be applied to the conditions قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة (gastric ulcer), خلل الحركة المتأخر (delayed motility disorder), فطار غزوي (aggressive breakfast), and تهديد بالإجهاض (threatened abortion). In particular, “chemically induced” is an allowed qualifier for تهديد بالإجهاض, indicating that this form of threatened abortion may arise as a result of chemical induction. Recognizing the chemically induced nature of these entities can aid clinicians in identifying underlying causes and tailoring appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2652
retrieved_docs.content: Gastric lavage, a procedure employed to cleanse the stomach, is clinically associated with secondary gastrointestinal bleeding, indicating that this intervention can be linked to or may precipitate bleeding within the gastrointestinal tract.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ and ربط البوق are medical concepts that frequently co‑occur, indicating that the presence of حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ is often associated with the finding or procedure ربط البوق. This consistent association suggests that clinicians should be aware of the link between these two entities, as recognizing one may prompt evaluation for the other, thereby informing diagnosis and guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2288
retrieved_docs.content: Antacids are agents that neutralize gastric acid and are classified within a broader pharmacological parent category that describes drug actions at the molecular level. In clinical practice, antacids are considered contraindicated when used together with famotidin, an H₂‑receptor antagonist, because concurrent administration can interfere with famotidin’s ability to reduce acid secretion and may lead to reduced therapeutic effectiveness or adverse interactions. Understanding that antacids belong to a parent group of molecular‑mechanism‑based drug effects, while also recognizing their contraindication with famotidin, helps clinicians select appropriate acid‑reducing therapies and avoid potential drug‑interaction pitfalls.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2469
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions are classified as a subcategory of اضطراب السبيل البولي (urinary tract disorder). These nonspecific genitourinary manifestations frequently co‑occur with حصاة حالبية (kidney stone), indicating that patients presenting with such symptoms should be evaluated for possible calculi within the urinary system. Recognizing that genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions belong to the broader umbrella of urinary tract disorders and are often associated with kidney stones helps clinicians prioritize diagnostic testing and management strategies aimed at identifying and treating underlying stone disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2622
retrieved_docs.content: Gastrointestinal hemorrhage is classified as a specific form of نَزِيف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي (upper gastrointestinal bleeding), indicating that it falls under the broader category of bleeding that originates in the upper portion of the digestive tract. Recognizing gastrointestinal hemorrhage as a subset of upper gastrointestinal bleeding helps clinicians contextualize its presentation, guide diagnostic evaluation, and tailor appropriate therapeutic interventions.
